infobox.value: (**DCMS**) is a [ministerial department](/wiki/Departments_of_the_Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Departments of the Government of the United Kingdom") of the [Government of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom"). It holds the responsibility for [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom") and [sport](/wiki/Sport_in_the_United_Kingdom "Sport in the United Kingdom"), and some aspects of the [media throughout the UK](/wiki/Media_of_the_United_Kingdom "Media of the United Kingdom"), such as [broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting"). Its main offices are at 100 [Parliament Street](/wiki/Whitehall "Whitehall"), occupying part of the building known as [Government Offices Great George Street](/wiki/Government_Offices_Great_George_Street "Government Offices Great George Street").

The **Department for Culture, Media and Sport** (**DCMS**) is a [ministerial department](/wiki/Departments_of_the_Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Departments of the Government of the United Kingdom") of the [Government of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom"). It holds the responsibility for [culture](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_Kingdom "Culture of the United Kingdom") and [sport](/wiki/Sport_in_the_United_Kingdom "Sport in the United Kingdom"), and some aspects of the [media throughout the UK](/wiki/Media_of_the_United_Kingdom "Media of the United Kingdom"), such as [broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting"). Its main offices are at 100 [Parliament Street](/wiki/Whitehall "Whitehall"), occupying part of the building known as [Government Offices Great George Street](/wiki/Government_Offices_Great_George_Street "Government Offices Great George Street").

It also has responsibility for the [tourism](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_United_Kingdom "Tourism in the United Kingdom"), [leisure](/wiki/Leisure_industry "Leisure industry") and [creative industries](/wiki/Creative_industries "Creative industries") (some jointly with the [Department for Business and Trade](/wiki/Department_for_Business_and_Trade "Department for Business and Trade")). The department was also responsible for the delivery of the [2012 Olympic Games](/wiki/2012_Olympic_Games "2012 Olympic Games") and [Paralympic Games](/wiki/2012_Paralympic_Games "2012 Paralympic Games").

From 2017 to 2023, the department had responsibility for the building of a [digital economy](/wiki/Digital_economy "Digital economy") and was known as the **Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport**.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The responsibilities for digital policy were transferred to the newly created [Department for Science, Innovation and Technology](/wiki/Department_for_Science,_Innovation_and_Technology "Department for Science, Innovation and Technology") in the [February 2023 cabinet reshuffle](/wiki/February_2023_British_cabinet_reshuffle "February 2023 British cabinet reshuffle").

DCMS originates from the **Department of National Heritage** (**DNH**), which itself was created on 11 April 1992 out of various other departments, soon after the [Conservative](/wiki/Second_Major_ministry "Second Major ministry") [election victory](/wiki/1992_United_Kingdom_general_election "1992 United Kingdom general election"). The former ministers for the [Arts](/wiki/Minister_for_the_Arts_(United_Kingdom) "Minister for the Arts (United Kingdom)") and for [Sport](/wiki/Minister_for_Sport_(United_Kingdom) "Minister for Sport (United Kingdom)") (both created in 1964) had previously been located in other departments.

DNH was renamed as the **Department for Culture, Media and Sport** (**DCMS**) on 14 July 1997, under the [premiership of Tony Blair](/wiki/Premiership_of_Tony_Blair "Premiership of Tony Blair"). It was renamed to **Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport** on 3 July 2017, staying **DCMS** under the [premiership of Theresa May](/wiki/Premiership_of_Theresa_May "Premiership of Theresa May") to reflect the department's increased activity in the digital sector.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The department was renamed back to the **Department for Culture, Media and Sport** (**DCMS**) in February 2023, with responsibility for digital moving to the new [Department for Science, Innovation and Technology](/wiki/Department_for_Science,_Innovation_and_Technology "Department for Science, Innovation and Technology").

### 2012 Olympics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 2012 Olympics")]

DCMS was the [co-ordinating department for the successful bid](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics_bids "2012 Summer Olympics bids") by [London to host the 2012 Olympics](/wiki/London_bid_for_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "London bid for the 2012 Summer Olympics") and appointed and oversees the agencies delivering the Games' infrastructure and programme, principally the [Olympic Delivery Authority](/wiki/Olympic_Delivery_Authority "Olympic Delivery Authority") (ODA) and [LOCOG](/wiki/LOCOG "LOCOG").

The [June 2007 Cabinet](/wiki/Brown_Ministry "Brown Ministry") [reshuffle](/wiki/Reshuffle "Reshuffle") led to [Tessa Jowell](/wiki/Tessa_Jowell "Tessa Jowell") MP taking on the role of [Paymaster General](/wiki/Paymaster_General "Paymaster General") and then [Minister for the Cabinet Office](/wiki/Minister_for_the_Cabinet_Office "Minister for the Cabinet Office") while remaining [Minister for the Olympics](/wiki/Minister_for_the_Olympics "Minister for the Olympics"). Ministerial responsibility for the Olympics was shared with Ms Jowell in the [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office"), but the staff of the [Government Olympic Executive](/wiki/Government_Olympic_Executive "Government Olympic Executive") (GOE) remained based in DCMS.

### 2010–2022

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2010–2022")]

Following the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"), ministerial responsibility for the Olympics returned to the Secretary of State. Although [Jeremy Hunt's](/wiki/Jeremy_Hunt_(politician) "Jeremy Hunt (politician)") full title was [Secretary of State for Culture, Olympics, Media and Sport](/wiki/Secretary_of_State_for_Culture,_Olympics,_Media_and_Sport "Secretary of State for Culture, Olympics, Media and Sport"), the department's name remained unchanged. On 4 September 2012, Hunt was appointed Health Secretary in a cabinet reshuffle and replaced by [Maria Miller](/wiki/Maria_Miller "Maria Miller"). Maria Miller later resigned due to controversy over her expenses. Her replacement was announced later that day as [Sajid Javid](/wiki/Sajid_Javid "Sajid Javid").

After the [2015 general election](/wiki/2015_United_Kingdom_general_election "2015 United Kingdom general election"), [John Whittingdale](/wiki/John_Whittingdale "John Whittingdale") was appointed as Secretary of State, tasked with initiating the [BBC Charter](/wiki/BBC_Charter "BBC Charter") review process. DCMS received full responsibility for the digital economy policy, formerly jointly held with [BIS](/wiki/Department_for_Business,_Innovation_and_Skills "Department for Business, Innovation and Skills"), and sponsorship of the [Information Commissioner's Office](/wiki/Information_Commissioner%27s_Office "Information Commissioner's Office") from the [Ministry of Justice](/wiki/Ministry_of_Justice_(United_Kingdom) "Ministry of Justice (United Kingdom)").

Whittingdale was replaced by [Karen Bradley](/wiki/Karen_Bradley "Karen Bradley") after the [referendum on the UK's membership of the EU](/wiki/2016_United_Kingdom_European_Union_membership_referendum "2016 United Kingdom European Union membership referendum") in July 2016. The **Office for Civil Society** moved from the [Cabinet Office](/wiki/Cabinet_Office "Cabinet Office") to DCMS as part of the same reshuffle.

In January 2018, [Matt Hancock](/wiki/Matt_Hancock "Matt Hancock"), previous Minister of State for Digital, was appointed Secretary of State as part of a Cabinet reshuffle. In the 9 July 2018 reshuffle, [Jeremy Wright](/wiki/Jeremy_Wright "Jeremy Wright") became the Secretary of State. [Nicky Morgan](/wiki/Nicky_Morgan "Nicky Morgan") became Secretary of State in July 2019; she stood down as an MP at the [2019 United Kingdom general election](/wiki/2019_United_Kingdom_general_election "2019 United Kingdom general election") but was ennobled as Baroness Morgan of Cotes and retained her position from within the [House of Lords](/wiki/House_of_Lords "House of Lords"). As part of the 13 February 2020 reshuffle, [Oliver Dowden](/wiki/Oliver_Dowden "Oliver Dowden") MP was appointed Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport. [Nadine Dorries](/wiki/Nadine_Dorries "Nadine Dorries") succeeded on 15 September 2021. In July 2022, Dorries personally granted [Grade II-listed status](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building") to a plaque of [Cecil Rhodes](/wiki/Cecil_Rhodes "Cecil Rhodes") which she believed is of "special historic interest".[[5]](#cite_note-theguardian1-5) This decision attracted controversy. On 5 September 2022, in anticipation of the appointment of [Liz Truss](/wiki/Liz_Truss "Liz Truss") as Prime Minister, Dorries tendered her resignation as culture secretary.

##### Other responsibilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Other responsibilities")]

Other responsibilities of DCMS include [listing of historic buildings](/wiki/Listed_building "Listed building"), [scheduling of ancient monuments](/wiki/Scheduled_monument "Scheduled monument"), export licensing of [cultural goods](/wiki/Cultural_industry "Cultural industry"), and management of the [Government Art Collection](/wiki/Government_Art_Collection "Government Art Collection") (GAC).

The Secretary of State has responsibility for the maintenance of the land and buildings making up the historic Royal Estate under the [Crown Lands Act 1851](/wiki/Crown_Lands_Act_1851 "Crown Lands Act 1851"). These inherited functions, which were once centralised in the [Office of Works](/wiki/Office_of_Works "Office of Works"), are now delivered as follows:

* The [Royal Parks](/wiki/Royal_Parks "Royal Parks") are maintained by an [executive agency](/wiki/Executive_agency "Executive agency") within DCMS, the [Royal Parks Agency](/wiki/Royal_Parks_Agency "Royal Parks Agency");
* The [unoccupied royal palaces in England](/wiki/List_of_British_royal_residences "List of British royal residences") are managed by a contract with [Historic Royal Palaces](/wiki/Historic_Royal_Palaces "Historic Royal Palaces");
* Maintenance of the [occupied royal palaces in England](/wiki/List_of_British_royal_residences "List of British royal residences") was funded by an annual [grant-in-aid](/wiki/Grant-in-aid "Grant-in-aid") to the [Royal Household](/wiki/Royal_Households_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal Households of the United Kingdom") until 31 March 2012. The Secretary of State for Culture retains legal responsibility for these palaces, but from 1 April 2012 this funding was amalgamated with the [Civil List](/wiki/Civil_List "Civil List") into a single [Sovereign Grant](/wiki/Sovereign_Grant_Act_2011 "Sovereign Grant Act 2011") administered by HM Treasury. DCMS continues to make a separate small grant to the [Royal Household](/wiki/Royal_Households_of_the_United_Kingdom "Royal Households of the United Kingdom") for the maintenance of [Marlborough House](/wiki/Marlborough_House "Marlborough House")

The department also has responsibility for state ceremonial occasions and royal funerals. However, responsibility for the [Civil List](/wiki/Civil_List "Civil List") element of head-of-state expenditure and income from the separate [Crown Estate](/wiki/Crown_Estate "Crown Estate") remains with the [Chancellor of the Exchequer](/wiki/Chancellor_of_the_Exchequer "Chancellor of the Exchequer").

DCMS works jointly with the [Department for Business, Innovation and Skills](/wiki/Department_for_Business,_Innovation_and_Skills "Department for Business, Innovation and Skills") (BIS) on [design](/wiki/Design "Design") issues, including sponsorship of the [Design Council](/wiki/Design_Council "Design Council"), and on relations with the [computer games](/wiki/Computer_games "Computer games") and [publishing industries](/wiki/Publishing_industry "Publishing industry").

DCMS works with [local community system providers](https://www.gov.uk/hmrc-internal-manuals/temporary-storage-of-imports/tsi004100) in broadcasting service via TV and Internet channels. In September 2022, it managed to hold a home office visit activity for media and data security knowledge sharing across different government departments.

DCMS organises the annual [Remembrance Day Ceremony](/wiki/Remembrance_Day "Remembrance Day") at the [Cenotaph](/wiki/Cenotaph "Cenotaph") and has responsibility for providing [humanitarian assistance](/wiki/Humanitarian_assistance "Humanitarian assistance") in the event of a [disaster](/wiki/Disaster "Disaster"). In the government's response to the [7 July 2005 London bombings](/wiki/7_July_2005_London_bombings "7 July 2005 London bombings") the department coordinated humanitarian support to the relatives of victims and arranged the memorial events.

DCMS has also supported cyber initiatives[[6]](#cite_note-6) such as [Cyber Discovery](/wiki/Cyber_Discovery "Cyber Discovery") and the UK Cyber Security Forum[[7]](#cite_note-7) to support innovation in the cyber industry.

DCMS formerly sponsored eight Regional Cultural Consortiums with NDPB status. In July 2008, DCMS announced that the consortiums would be phased out over a twelve-month period and replaced by a new alliance of the regional teams of Arts Council England, Sport England, English Heritage and the [Museums, Libraries and Archives Council](/wiki/Museums,_Libraries_and_Archives_Council "Museums, Libraries and Archives Council") (MLA).

## Devolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Devolution")]

Culture, sport and tourism are [devolved](/wiki/Devolved "Devolved") matters, with responsibility resting with corresponding departments in the [Scottish Government](/wiki/Scottish_Government "Scottish Government") in [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland"), the [Welsh Government](/wiki/Welsh_Government "Welsh Government") in [Wales](/wiki/Wales "Wales") and the [Northern Ireland Executive](/wiki/Northern_Ireland_Executive "Northern Ireland Executive") in [Northern Ireland](/wiki/Northern_Ireland "Northern Ireland").

Media-related policy is generally [reserved](/wiki/Reserved_and_excepted_matters "Reserved and excepted matters") to Westminster i.e. not devolved. These areas include:

### Scotland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Scotland")]

Reserved matters:[[11]](#cite_note-11)

* [Film classification](/wiki/Film_censorship "Film censorship")
* [Broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting")
* [Public lending right](/wiki/Public_lending_right "Public lending right")
* [Entertainment](/wiki/Entertainment "Entertainment") [licensing](/wiki/Licensing "Licensing")
* [National Lottery](/wiki/National_Lottery_(United_Kingdom) "National Lottery (United Kingdom)")
* [press freedom](/wiki/Freedom_of_press "Freedom of press") and [regulation](/wiki/Regulation "Regulation")

Scotland's [comparability factor](/wiki/Barnett_formula "Barnett formula") (the proportion of spending in this area devolved to the Scottish Government) was **68%** for 2021/22.[[12]](#cite_note-CBP-12)

### Wales

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Wales")]

Reserved matters:[[13]](#cite_note-13)

* [Film classification](/wiki/Film_censorship "Film censorship")
* [Broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting"), [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC")
* [Public lending right](/wiki/Public_lending_right "Public lending right")
* [Entertainment](/wiki/Entertainment "Entertainment") [licensing](/wiki/Licensing "Licensing")
* Betting, Gambling and Lotteries
* [press freedom](/wiki/Freedom_of_press "Freedom of press") and [regulation](/wiki/Regulation "Regulation")

Wales' [comparability factor](/wiki/Barnett_formula "Barnett formula") (the proportion of spending in this area devolved to the Welsh Government) was **67.7%** for 2021/22.[[12]](#cite_note-CBP-12)

### Northern Ireland

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Department_for_Culture,_Media_and_Sport&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Northern Ireland")]

Reserved matters:[[14]](#cite_note-14)

* [Broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting")
* [National Lottery](/wiki/National_Lottery_(United_Kingdom) "National Lottery (United Kingdom)")

The department's main counterparts in Northern Ireland are as follows:[[15]](#cite_note-15)

* [Department for Communities](/wiki/Department_for_Communities "Department for Communities") (architecture, arts, culture, galleries, gambling, historic built environment, libraries, liquor licensing, museums, sport)
* [Department for the Economy](/wiki/Department_for_the_Economy "Department for the Economy") (tourism)

Northern Ireland's [comparability factor](/wiki/Barnett_formula "Barnett formula") (the proportion of spending in this area devolved to the Northern Ireland Executive) was **69.9%** for 2021/22.[[12]](#cite_note-CBP-12)
